race.name: Bike MS: New York City
race.tagline: The flagship edition of the world's largest cycling fundraising series. 30 or 50 traffic-free miles around Manhattan — through Battery Park Tunnel, past the Brooklyn Bridge, across the George Washington Bridge — raising money to end multiple sclerosis.

vitals.location: Manhattan, New York City, New York, USA
vitals.registration: Online via events.nationalmssociety.org. Registration fee $50 (increases to $70 after September 20). $250 fundraising minimum required.
vitals.feed_zones: Rest stops along the route with water, snacks, and support. Mechanical and medical support available on course. Full celebration at Pier 76 finish area.
vitals.cutoff_time: Not published. This is a supported charity ride — all riders are supported to finish.
route_options: 30-mile route: Traffic-free loop around Manhattan via East and West Side Highways and Battery Park Tunnel
50-mile route: Manhattan loop plus George Washington Bridge crossing into New Jersey and climb up Route 9W

climate.primary: New York City mid-October — cool autumn conditions
climate.description: Mid-October in New York City delivers classic autumn weather. Average highs of 60-66F (16-19C) with morning lows around 50F (10C). The 7:30 AM start can feel crisp, especially along the Hudson River waterfront. By midday, conditions are ideal for cycling — cool, dry air with moderate sun. October averages about 3.1 inches of rain with roughly a 32% chance of precipitation on any given day. The fall foliage along the Palisades on the 50-mile route adds visual drama to the New Jersey section.
challenges: Cool morning start temperatures (48-55F) along the Hudson River waterfront — dress in layers
Wind exposure on the George Washington Bridge and along the river corridors
Possible autumn rain — event runs rain or shine
Early sunset (around 6:15 PM) limits afternoon flexibility

terrain.primary: Flat traffic-free urban roads with one significant climb (50-mile route)
terrain.surface: Paved roads and highways throughout. The 30-mile route follows the East and West Side Highways around Manhattan — flat, wide, and traffic-free with NYPD escort. The route passes through the Battery Park Tunnel at Manhattan's southern tip. The 50-mile route adds a crossing of the George Washington Bridge and a climb up Route 9W through the New Jersey Palisades — the only significant elevation on either route.
features: West Side Highway — car-free cycling corridor along the Hudson River
Battery Park Tunnel — riding through the tunnel at Manhattan's southern tip
FDR Drive / East Side — northbound along the East River with skyline views
Harlem River Drive — connecting the East and West sides of upper Manhattan
George Washington Bridge crossing (50-mile route) — iconic bridge with Manhattan skyline views
Route 9W climb through the Palisades (50-mile route) — the only real climbing on either route

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 57. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 57.

course_description.character: An urban charity ride that transforms Manhattan's busiest corridors into a traffic-free cycling course. The 30-mile route is a complete loop around Manhattan — south down the West Side Highway, through the Battery Park Tunnel at the island's southern tip, north up the FDR Drive and East Side, across Harlem River Drive, and back south along 10th Avenue to the Pier 76 finish. It is flat, scenic, and fully escorted by NYPD. The 50-mile route adds 20 miles by crossing the George Washington Bridge into New Jersey and climbing Route 9W through the Palisades — the only real climbing on either route — before returning over the GWB to rejoin the Manhattan loop. Both routes start and finish at Pier 76 on the West Side Highway between 35th and 36th streets.
course_description.signature_challenge: There is no signature physical challenge. The signature experience is the cause — riding alongside people living with MS, their families, and their teams through traffic-free Manhattan. The 50-mile route's George Washington Bridge crossing and Palisades climb add genuine effort, but the emotional weight of riding for MS is heavier than any gradient.
suffering_zones: FDR Drive & East Side — The River Run
George Washington Bridge — The Crossing (50-mile route)

logistics.airport: JFK, LaGuardia (LGA), or Newark (EWR) — all within 30-60 minutes of Midtown Manhattan by transit. LaGuardia is closest to the Pier 76 start area.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Packet pickup is at the Courtyard by Marriott New York Manhattan/Midtown West (461 W 34th St.) — staying in the West 30s-40s near the Hudson puts you walking distance from both packet pickup and the Pier 76 start. Midtown West hotels run $200-400/night in October. Hell's Kitchen and Chelsea offer slightly better rates with easy subway access. For budget options, consider Brooklyn or Queens hotels with subway connections to 34th Street.
logistics.food: It is New York City. Pre-ride: grab a bagel from any of the thousands of delis within walking distance of Pier 76. Rest stops along the route provide water and snacks. Post-ride celebration at Pier 76 with food and festivities overlooking the Hudson River. Hell's Kitchen restaurants are steps from the start/finish.

history.origin_story: Bike MS began in 1980 when Will Munger, executive director of the Minnesota Chapter of the National MS Society, and his 10-year-old son rode their bicycles from Duluth to Minneapolis. Inspired by their success, Munger organized the first official Bike MS ride — 200 cyclists rode from Minneapolis to Duluth, raising $33,000. By 1981, other chapters heard about the Minnesota ride and started their own. In 1985, the National MS Society adopted Bike MS as an official event to be held in every chapter. The New York City edition launched in the mid-1980s and celebrated its 40th anniversary in 2024. Today, Bike MS is the largest cycling fundraising series in the world — 75,000 annual riders across approximately 70 events nationwide — with total lifetime fundraising exceeding $1.4 billion for MS research and services.
notable_moments: 1980: First Bike MS ride — 200 cyclists, Minneapolis to Duluth, $33,000 raised
1985: National MS Society officially adopts Bike MS as a nationwide event series
Mid-1980s: New York City edition launches, growing into the series' flagship urban event
2024: Bike MS NYC celebrates 40 years of cycling the Big Apple
Series milestone: Bike MS riders have raised over $1.4 billion for the National MS Society since 1980
2026: Bike MS NYC scheduled for October 18 at Pier 76, Manhattan
history.reputation: The flagship urban edition of the world's largest cycling fundraising series. Where Manhattan's highways go car-free and thousands ride together to end multiple sclerosis.

biased_opinion.summary: Bike MS: New York City is the flagship edition of the world's largest cycling fundraising series, wrapped in one of the world's most recognizable backdrops. For one October morning, Manhattan's highways and tunnels go car-free and belong to cyclists raising money to end multiple sclerosis. The 30-mile loop around Manhattan is flat, scenic, and accessible to anyone who can ride a bicycle. The 50-mile route adds the George Washington Bridge and the Palisades climb for riders wanting genuine effort. Neither route is about the cycling — both are about the cause. Since 1980, Bike MS riders nationwide have raised over $1.4 billion for the National MS Society. The NYC edition puts that mission against the backdrop of the world's most famous skyline, the Battery Park Tunnel, the Hudson River, and thousands of people riding for someone they love.
strengths: The cause: every dollar raised funds MS research and services through the National MS Society. $1.4 billion raised since 1980 across the Bike MS series.
The setting: traffic-free Manhattan in October. Battery Park Tunnel, both waterfronts, George Washington Bridge — NYC landmarks from the saddle with NYPD escort.
Accessibility: $50-70 registration and $250 fundraising minimum make this one of the most affordable major charity rides in America.
The community: deeply emotional cause-driven atmosphere. Riders living with MS, families, teams united by a mission larger than cycling.
weaknesses: Not competitive: zero racing element. If you need a timing chip, look elsewhere.
Flat and short: the 30-mile route is a leisurely morning. Even the 50-mile route is not a serious physical test.
NYC costs for travelers: the event is cheap but the city is not. Hotels, flights, and meals add up.
The 8:00 AM hard close on route access is unforgiving for latecomers.
biased_opinion.bottom_line: This is not a bike ride you do for the cycling. It is a bike ride you do for the people in your life who are fighting multiple sclerosis. The Manhattan setting makes it scenic. The cause makes it meaningful. If you have a connection to MS — and millions of Americans do — Bike MS NYC is where you put that connection into motion.

final_verdict.one_liner: The world's largest cycling fundraising series meets the world's most famous skyline. Traffic-free Manhattan for multiple sclerosis.
final_verdict.should_you_race: You do not race Bike MS — you ride it for a cause. Yes if you want a traffic-free cycling experience through Manhattan with full NYPD escort while raising money for MS research. Yes if you have a connection to multiple sclerosis and want to channel it into something physical and communal. The 50-mile route adds the George Washington Bridge and Palisades climbing for stronger riders. Skip if you need competitive cycling, serious distance, or technical terrain.
final_verdict.alternatives: If you want a larger NYC cycling experience: try the TD Five Boro Bike Tour (32,000 riders, all 5 boroughs). If you want a more demanding charity ride with deeper emotional weight: try the Pan-Mass Challenge (177 miles, $6,000 fundraising commitment). If you want a competitive ride in the NYC area: try GFNY New York.

videos.title: Bicycling from the Big Apple to Boston (250 miles, 2 days)
videos.description:
Thank you for watching me bike 250 miles from Manhattan, NY to Boston, MA in two days.

Here is the path created by BikewithGPS user, Brandon Mak, that I took with some Apple map modifications: https://ridewithgps.com/routes/39336535

Strava details:
Day 1: https://www.strava.com/activities/9863368143
Day 2: https://www.strava.com/activities/9870410656

videos.title: GFNY NYC 2023. Race video.
videos.transcript:
welcome to the 2023 edition of the gfny
world championship in New York City
the gfny world championship is truly an
International race with thousands of
riders coming from all over the planet
with new cash prizes for intermediate
Sprints and an updated course
[Music]
[Applause]
it's also the finale of the gfny global
Endurance Sports series which Now
features an incredible 40 races
worldwide this year the race was faster
and more competitive than ever Pro and
amateur cyclists left off the starting
line on the spectacular George
Washington Bridge and zipped through
Fort Lee and into Palisades Interstate
Park the Sparks flew again on the
climate Alpine as Italian matiagafuri
last year's second place finisher
casually tested his legs he jumped to
the front of the race taking the Sprint
cream at the top but once there gaffordi
sat up there was plenty of Road left and
he was happy to let his SWAT club
teammates do some of the hard work
controlling the race the first serious
attack came with 100K still to go
Colombian Jaime Vergara was quickly
joined by Miguel Arroyo from Mexico but
SWAT club wasn't going to let them get
too far a pack of nearly 30 riders with
all the race favorites was close behind
got four divided time while teammates
like David minote and Francesco Zant
Tedeschi helped keep the Gap to the
leaders to less than a minute
and then it was him alone against the
Peloton American time trial champ Ben
Wolf launched the charge that finally
kicked the footy into action seeing an
opportunity South African Alexander
wurzdale briefly jumped onto furry's
wheel and then hung on for dear life but
by the summit of cheese coat all the
challengers had faded from memory it was
now Jessica furry on a long solo drive
to the finish the disorganized Chase
group somewhere down the road
dusted the field in a time of 327-32
nearly seven minutes faster than the
second and third place finishers
in the women's race it was a similarly
dominant performance Italian Kiara dhoni
also from SWAT club LED from start to
finish obviously SWAT club came to win
but coming in second and third was still
a cause for celebration for the Italians
Annalisa Prato and last year's champ
Samantha Arnaldo they finished the way
they started together with big smiles on
their faces at the Finish Riders
collected their medals before reuniting
with friends and family at the gfny
Finnish Village in Fort Lee
gfny is always an amazing event music
food drinks and of course prizes the
overall men's and women's Champions were
awarded a spectacular all-expense paid
trip to the next gfny NYC World
Championships the first six finishers
will also race the new UCI Pro Race
Grand Premio gfny next year as part of
team gfmy
the next day some of the top Riders
gathered on one of the towers of the
George Washington Bridge for a very
special gfny honor a view Fit For A
Champion for the gfny world championship
thanks again for watching and don't wait
sign up today for a gfny race India Pro
for a day
foreign

videos.title: GFNY New York 2019 Narrado! 4to general y 1ro cat. 🏆
videos.description:
Cómo se vive un GFNY de New York desde el pelotón del frente? Qué se necesita para hacer un podio? En el video les explico y les muestro todo! Un poco largo pero prometo que vale la pena!

► Sígueme por estos links!:
+ Instagram: http://instagram.com/danielhernandezbc
+ Strava: https://www.strava.com/athletes/danielhernandezbc
+ Zwift: con la App Zwift Companion busca Daniel Hernandez BC [R56]

► Productos que USO y recomiendo:
- ANT+: https://amzn.to/2S34WpS

- Favero Assioma Duo: https://amzn.to/2VXedko

- Micrófono: https://amzn.to/3aSX6Wo
- Bandana: https://amzn.to/35yXcBz
- Cleats pedales: https://amzn.to/3cKqj73

► Recuerden suscribirse y dejar un comentario si les gustó!

► PREGUNTAS FRECUENTES:
- Eres profesional? No, soy recreativo y empecé a montar en el 2015, y tengo un trabajo de oficina normal. 
- Qué rodillo usas? Tengo un Wahoo Kickr 2018
- Qué edad tienes? 32 años
- Cuánto pesas? Usualmente entre 70 y 74kg 
- Cual es tu FTP? Alrededor de 350w.
- Cuando sacas mas videos? Sigueme en instagram @DanielHernandezBC para conocer más.

► Soy un ciclista amateur, amante de la competencia limpia y de buscar estar en la mejor forma posible con las limitaciones de tiempo, cuerpo y mente que tenga. He corrido en muchas carreras en lo que llevo montando, desde campeonatos nacionales Máster, hasta el GFNY New York, donde quedé de 4to en la general y 1ro en mi categoría. Pertenezco al equipo Ruta 56 y Marathon Team MTB.

► Este canal de YouTube lo uso para subir carreras de bici con narraciones, explicando las estrategias usadas, para que aprendan lo más posible con mis experiencias. También pueden encontrar tutoriales y varios tips sobre ciclismo de temas que valen la pena compartir.

----
► Suscríbete a mi canal aquí!: http://www.youtube.com/c/DanielHernandezBC?sub_confirmation=1
----

videos.title: 100 Pros Vs 5000 Amateurs | Who Will Win In NYC?
videos.description:
Can 100 pros hold off 5000 amateur cyclists? Join us at Gran Fondo New York, where we put Alex on the pro Jamison Racing team, and Ollie, riding in the GFNY amateur peloton, to the test! Is there really such a big difference between amateur and pro cyclists? 

In collaboration with @GFNYWorld  and @colnagoTV 
👉 https://gfny.com/
👉 https://www.colnago.com/

Who will win in Gran Fondo New York? 0:00
What is  GFNY? 0:31
Our bikes: The Colnago V4RS & The C68 0:57
The start line! 4:30 a.m. 1:46
GO TIME! 2:56
Why aren’t we in our usual kit? 3:32  
First climb: The Alpine 4:32
Being a pro for the day: Jamison Racing Team 5:28
Checking in with Ollie 6:39
Alex’s experience with the pros 8:03
The aftermath 10:41
Could 100 pros REALLY hold off 5000 amateurs? 11:01

The Global Cycling Network (GCN) is the largest online cycling channel in the world, bringing together a global community of road cyclists to celebrate everything that’s great about the world of cycling.

Our videos bring fans compelling daily content including expert tutorials, techniques, training, racing, cutting-edge bike tech, unparalleled behind the scenes event coverage, humour,  entertainment, and more.

Presented by ex-pro riders, GCN offers a uniquely qualified insight into the world of cycling, and most importantly it’s fuelled by our passionate and enthusiastic fans – everyone who makes up the GCN community. We also bring the latest and greatest tech to your attention, showcase the best places in the world to ride and get exclusive access to events and races.

Welcome to the Global Cycling Network | Inside Cycling

videos.curation_reason: High-quality production featuring both pro and amateur perspectives of GFNY NYC with detailed course description and race experience.

quotes.text: The 135 km route starts on the iconic George Washington Bridge and then loops up north out of the city. The course is lumpy with several short climbs littered throughout amounting to over 2,000 M of elevation, kind of like an Ardennes classic.

Pro and amateur cyclists left off the starting line on the spectacular George Washington Bridge and zipped through Fort Lee and into Palisades Interstate Park. The sparks flew again on the climb at Alpine.

key_challenges.name: Alpine climb
key_challenges.description: First categorized climb at just 1.6 km but already creating a selection of just 40 riders from the massive field. The pace was described as rapid.

key_challenges.name: Cheese coat
key_challenges.description: A summit climb where challengers faded from memory, described as a point where the race was decided.

terrain_notes.text: The parkour is lumpy with several short climbs littered throughout amounting to over 2,000 m of elevation, kind of like an Ardennes classic.

race_day_tips.text: All participants must wear the official GFNY jersey during the race.
There's a huge drafting benefit from the ginormous amateur peloton that makes faster times very possible.

additional_quotes.text: It's 4:30 a.m. we're riding through the streets of New York and it's like what an experience to have no cars anywhere but also slightly terrifying knowing what lies ahead.
This event is for all abilities and just finishing it is a massive achievement.
GFNY is always an amazing event music food drinks and of course prizes at the GFNY finish village in Fort Lee.

rider_intel.search_text: GFNY New York is a 135km race starting on the iconic George Washington Bridge that attracts thousands of international riders. The course is lumpy with several short climbs totaling over 2,000m of elevation, similar to an Ardennes classic. Key challenges include the Alpine climb at 18km which creates early selections from the massive field, and the Cheese coat summit around 100km where races are often decided. The route goes through Fort Lee into Palisades Interstate Park before looping north of the city. Riders describe the pace as rapid from early on, with the first categorized climb at Alpine reducing the field to just 40 riders despite its short 1.6km length. The event features both UCI pro and amateur races, with the massive amateur peloton providing significant drafting benefits. All participants must wear official GFNY jerseys. The race finishes with a celebration village in Fort Lee featuring music, food, drinks and prizes.
